title: Advance Passenger Information Act, 2020 brought into force (NOTICE)
text:
VIRGIN ISLANDS
STATUTORY INSTRUMENT 2022 NO. 104
ADVANCE PASSENGER INFORMATION ACT, 2020
(No. 12 of 2020)
A NOTICE
MADE BY THE MINISTER RESPONSIBLE FOR IMMIGRATION UNDER SECTION
1(2) OF THE ADVANCE PASSENGER INFORMATION ACT, 2020 DECLARING THE
DATE ON WHICH THE SAID ACT SHALL COME INTO FORCE
[Gazetted 1st December, 2022]
WHEREAS it is provided by section 1(2) of the Advance Passenger Information Act, 2020
(No. 12 of 2020) that the said Act shall come into force on such date as the Minister may, by Notice
published in the Gazette, appoint;
NOW THEREFORE, in exercise of the powers vested in me, I hereby appoint the 1st day
of December, 2022 to be the date on which the Advance Passenger Information Act, 2020 shall
come into force.
Made by the Minister this 30th day of December, 2022.
(Sgd.) Natalio D. Wheatley,
Minister responsible for Immigration.

title: Police (Amendment) Act, 2023
text:
No. 2 of 2023
VIRGIN ISLANDS
POLICE (AMENDMENT) ACT, 2023
ARRANGEMENT OF SECTIONS
SECTION
1. Short title and commencement
2. New section 35A inserted
1

No. 2 of 2023 Police (Amendment) Act, 2023 Virgin
Islands
I ASSENT
(Sgd.) John Rankin CMG,
Governor.
20th March, 2023
VIRGIN ISLANDS
No. 2 of 2023
AN ACT TO AMEND THE POLICE ACT, REVISED EDITION 2013, AND TO PROVIDE FOR
MATTERS CONCERNING THEREWITH.
[Gazetted 21st March, 2023]
ENACTED by the Legislature of the Virgin Islands as follows:
Short title and commencement
1. (1) This Act may be cited as the Police (Amendment) Act, 2023.
(2) This Act shall come into force on such date as the Governor may, by
Proclamation published in the Gazette, appoint.
New section 35A inserted
2. The Police Act, Revised Edition 2013, is amended by inserting
immediately after section 35, the following new section:
“Power to collect intimate samples
35A (1) Except as provided by this section, a member of the Force
shall not take or record an intimate sample from a person without the
consent of that person.
(2) Consent to the taking of an intimate sample by a person
shall be recorded either in writing or in an audio or audio-visual
recording.
(3) Where a person refuses to consent to the taking of his or
her intimate sample under subsection (1), a member of the Force may
take an intimate sample, without consent of such person, subject to an
order providing for an intimate sample to be taken signed by a
Magistrate or a Judge of the High Court.
(4) A member of the Force seeking an order for the taking of an
intimate sample shall apply to A Magistrate or Judge in writing, and
2

shall state the offence being investigated and detail his or her grounds
for believing that:
(a) the person from whom the intimate sample is sought is
involved in an arrestable offence; and
(b) the intimate sample will tend to prove or disprove the
involvement of the person from whom the intimate
sample is sought, in the arrestable offence being
investigated.
(5) A Judge or Magistrate, may only make an order for the taking
of an intimate sample where he or she:
(a) has reasonable grounds for suspecting that the person
from whom the intimate sample is sought is involved in
an arrestable offence; and
(b) is satisfied that the intimate sample will tend to prove or
disprove the involvement of the person from whom the
intimate sample is sought, in the arrestable offence being
investigated.
(6) The reasons for granting the order mentioned in subsection (5)
above shall be contained in writing and shall be disclosed to the person
against whom the order is granted.
(7) Where an order of the Court has been given and it is proposed
that an intimate sample shall be taken in pursuance of the order, a
member of the Force shall inform the person from whom the sample is
to be taken
(a) of the nature of the offence in the commission of which it
is suspected that the person has been involved;
(b) of the order granted by the Court; and
(c) the grounds or reasons for the granting of the Order by the
Court pursuant to subsection (6);
and such matters under paragraphs (a) to (c) shall be recorded
immediately after the sample is taken.
(8) Where an order is granted for the taking of an intimate sample
or where consent is given, in the case of an intimate sample which is a
dental impression, the sample may be taken from a person only by a
dental practitioner.
(9) Where an order is granted for the taking of an intimate sample
or where consent is given, except in the case of a sample of urine or
buccal swab, the sample may be taken from a person only by a
(a) registered medical practitioner; or
(b) a registered nurse.
(10) For the purposes of this Act an intimate sample shall not be
taken from a child under this Act, except
3

(a) where consent has been obtained in writing or in an audio
or audio-visual recording from a parent or guardian of the
child; or
(b) in the absence of such consent under paragraph (a), an
order of the Court authorising the taking of a sample from
the child is obtained in accordance with subsection (5).
(11) In circumstances where a person from whom an intimate
sample is taken
(a) is acquitted;
(b) is not charged; or
(c) is charged, but the prosecution does not proceed with the
charge or the proceedings are discontinued,
the intimate sample taken from him and her and any evidence, records
or documentation connected thereto shall, after the decision is taken
not to charge the said person or upon the discharge or acquittal of the
person, be destroyed in the presence of a Gazetted Officer or Inspector.
(12) The Commissioner shall provide or cause to be provided to
the person to whom subsection (11) applies, written confirmation that
the intimate sample, all evidence, records or documentation in relation
thereto have been destroyed, including confirmation that the facility or
service provider which analyses intimate samples shall also destroy any
such evidence, records or documentation or other such records.
(13) For the avoidance of doubt under subsection (11) and (12),
“documentation” includes analysis, reports and other related items, but
does not include court documents such as warrants or orders.
(14) Nothing in this section applies to any person
(a) arrested or detained under the Counter-Terrorism Act,
2021, No. 33 of 2021; or
(b) arrested under an extradition arrest power.
(15) For the purposes of this section unless the context otherwise
requires
“arrestable offence” means an offence prescribed as such in
Schedule 1 to the Criminal Code, Revised Edition
2013.
“child” has the meaning as assigned under section 2 of the
Children and Young Persons Act, Revised Edition
2013;
“dental practitioner” has the meaning as assigned under
section 2 of the Medical, Dental and Allied Health
Practitioners Act, 2000, No. 4 of 2000;
“guardian” has the meaning as assigned under section 2 of the
Children and Young Persons Act, Revised Edition
2013;
“intimate sample” means
4

(a) a sample of
(i) blood;
(ii) semen;
(iii) any other tissue fluid;
(iv) urine;
(v) saliva; or
(vi) public hair;
(b) a dental impression; or
(c) a swab taken from any part of a person’s genitals or
from a person’s body orifices other than the mouth.
“Medical practitioner” has the meaning as assigned under
section 2 of the Medical, Dental and Allied Health
Practitioners Act, 2000, No. 4 of 2000; and
“nurse” has the meaning as assigned under section 2 of the
Nurses and Midwives Act, 2020, No. 25 of 2020.
Passed by the House of Assembly this 9th day of March, 2023.
(Sgd.) Corine N. George-Massicote,
Speaker.
(Sgd.) Phyllis Evans,
Clerk of the House of Assembly.
5

title: Immigration and Passport (Amendment) Act, 2023

VIRGIN ISLANDS
No. 22 of 2023
AN ACT TO AMEND THE IMMIGRATION AND PASSPORT ACT, REVISED EDITION 2013,
TO CLARIFY THE APPLICATION OF CERTAIN PROVISIONS TO THE GRANT OF
CERTIFICATES OF BELONGER STATUS PURSUANT TO SECTION 16(5A); AND FOR
RELATED MATTERS.
[Gazetted 19th April , 2023]
ENACTED by the Legislature of the Virgin Islands as follows:
Short title
1. This Act may be cited as the Immigration and Passport (Amendment) Act,
2023.
Section 16 amended
2. Section 16 of the Immigration and Passport Act, Revised Edition 2013, is
amended
(a) in subsection (2) by inserting after paragraph (b) the following
paragraph:
“(ba) there has been an exercise in relation to the person of the
power set out in subsection (5a); or”; and
(b) in subsection (3) by deleting the words “subsection (4)” and
substituting the words “ subsections (4) and (5a)”.
2

Section 17 amended
3. Section 17(1) of the Immigration and Passport Act, Revised Edition 2013,
is amended by inserting after the words “section 16,” the words “except for a
certificate granted under section 16(5a),”.
Passed by the House of Assembly this 17th day of April, 2023.
(Sgd.) Corine N. George-Massicote,

Section 16 2. The Immigration and Passport Act (referred to in this Act as “the
amended. principal Act”) is amended in section 16

Revised edition

discretion, permit such person in writing to land and remain in the

to land by

Section 2 (a) and 6. (1) Section 2(a) and (b) and section 3 of this Act shall cease to apply

title: Immigration and Passport (Amendment) Act, 2016
text:
No. 10 of 2016
VIRGIN ISLANDS
IMMIGRATION AND PASSPORT (AMENDMENT) ACT, 2016
ARRANGEMENT OF SECTIONS
Section
1. Short title.
2. Section 31 amended.
3. Section 32 amended.
1

No. 10 of 2016 Immigration and Passport (Amendment) Act, 2016 Virgin
Islands
I Assent
(Sgd.) John S. Duncan, OBE,
Governor
17th May, 2016
VIRGIN ISLANDS
No. 10 of 2016
An Act to amend the Immigration and Passport Ordinance (Cap. 130).
[Gazetted 23rd May, 2016]
ENACTED by the Legislature of the Virgin Islands as follows:
Short title. 1. This Act may be cited as the Immigration and Passport (Amendment) Act, 2016.
Section 31 2. The Immigration and Passport Ordinance (referred to in this Act as “the principal
amended. Ordinance”) is amended in section 31
Cap. 130
(a) in subsection (1),
(i) by inserting immediately after the word “grant” the words “or
extend”;
(ii) by deleting paragraph (b) and replacing it with the following new
paragraph:
“(b) to remain in the Territory for the period specified in the
permit for the purpose of engaging in the gainful
occupation specified in the permit;”;
(iii) by inserting immediately after paragraph (b) the following new
paragraph:
“(c) to remain in the Territory for the period specified in the
permit for the purpose of seeking to be engaged in a gainful
occupation.”.
2

(b) by inserting immediately after subsection (1), the following subsections:
“(1A) The Chief Immigration Officer may grant a permit pursuant
to subsection (1)(c), where the person applying for that permit
(a) has resigned from his or her employment, or was
dismissed from his or her employment, as the case may
be and has held valid permits to engage in gainful
occupation, for at least five years, preceding the
date of the application; or
(b) has held a valid permit to reside in the Territory other
than for the purpose of engaging in any gainful
occupation, for at least three years, preceding the date
of the application.
(2A) The Chief Immigration Officer may, in exceptional
circumstances, grant a permit referred to in subsection (1) (c) to a person
who does not satisfy the time limits prescribed in subsection (1A).”.
Section 32 3. Section 32 of the principal Ordinance is amended
amended.
(a) in subsection (1)(b) and (c), by inserting immediately after the words
“the Minister may” the words “in writing”; and
(b) by inserting after subsection (2) the following new subsection
“(3) Subject to the written approval of the Minister and upon
application being made by the permit holder in the prescribed form, the
Chief Immigration Officer may vary any conditions or restrictions
endorsed on a permit granted under section 31.”.
Passed by the House of Assembly this 20th day of April, 2016.
(Sgd.) Ingrid Moses-Scatliffe,
Speaker.
(Sgd.) Phyllis Evans,
Clerk of the House of Assembly.
3

title: Customs Management and Duties (Amendment) Act, 2023
text:
No. 18 of 2023
VIRGIN ISLANDS
CUSTOMS MANAGEMENT AND DUTIES
(AMENDMENT) ACT, 2023
ARRANGEMENT OF SECTIONS
SECTION
1. Short title and commencement
2. New Part XIIA inserted
1

No. 18 of 2023 Customs Management and Duties Virgin
(Amendment) Act, 2023 Islands
I ASSENT
(Sgd.) John Rankin CMG,
Governor.
27th March, 2023
VIRGIN ISLANDS
No. 18 of 2023
AN ACT TO AMEND THE CUSTOMS MANAGEMENT AND DUTIES ACT,
2010, NO. 6 OF 2010, AND TO PROVIDE FOR MATTERS CONCERNING
THEREWITH.
[Gazetted 28th March, 2023]
ENACTED by the Legislature of the Virgin Islands as follows:
Short title and commencement
1. (1) This Act may be cited as the Customs Management and Duties
(Amendment) Act, 2023.
(2) This Act shall come into force on such date as the Minister may, by
Notice published in the Gazette, appoint.
New Part XIIA inserted
2. The Customs Management and Duties Act, 2010 (No. 6 of 2010), is
amended by inserting immediately after section 142, the following new Part:
“PART XIIA
COOPERATION WITH LAW ENFORCEMENT AGENCIES,
COMPETENT AUTHORITIES AND FOREIGN LAW
ENFORCEMENT AGENCIES
Duty to cooperate
142A. (1) Subject to the provisions of this Act, the Commissioner may,
upon request in writing or on his own volition, co-operate with:
(a) any law enforcement agency in the Territory;
(b) a competent authority acting pursuant to an enactment;
or
(c) any foreign law enforcement agency,
for the purposes of
2

(i) investigating an offence;
(ii) assisting with or facilitating any investigating of
an offence; or
(iii) ensuring compliance with a request from a law
enforcement agency in the Territory, a competent
authority or a foreign law enforcement agency,
acting pursuant to an enactment.
(2) Co-operation for the purposes of this section may include
(a) the sharing of documents and information which the
Commissioner is not prevented by this Act or any other
enactment from disclosing; or
(b) making requests for assistance to any law enforcement
agency in the Territory, competent authority or foreign
law enforcement agency acting pursuant to an
enactment.
(3) Where, pursuant to a request for assistance made in
accordance with this section, any information, document or other
material is received, the Commissioner shall, if requested in writing by
the authority that provided the information, document or other material,
provide in a timely manner feedback to that authority on the use and
usefulness of the information, document or other material received.
(4) The Minister upon consultation with the Commissioner
may issue such guidelines as may be required for the procedures and
measures in relation to cooperation with law enforcement agencies,
competent authorities and foreign law enforcement agencies for the
purposes of this section.
(5) For the purposes of this section, “foreign law
enforcement agencies” means any authority in a jurisdiction outside the
Virgin Islands which exercises a power or performs a function
corresponding or similar to a power or function exercised or performed
by Customs, as established under the Customs Management and Duties
Act, 2010, No. 6 of 2010.”.
Passed by the House of Assembly this 10th day of March, 2023.
(Sgd.) Corine N. George-Massicote,
Speaker.
(Sgd.) Phyllis Evans,
Clerk of the House of Assembly.
3

title: Criminal Justice International Co-operation (Amendment) Act, 2023
text:
No. 14 of 2023
VIRGIN ISLANDS
CRIMINAL JUSTICE (INTERNATIONAL CO-OPERATION)
(AMENDMENT) ACT, 2023
ARRANGEMENT OF SECTIONS
SECTION
1. Short title and commencement
2. Section 4 amended
3. Section 5 amended
4. Section 5B repealed
5. Section 6 repealed
6. Section 11 amended
1

No. 14 of 2023 Criminal Justice (International Co-operation Virgin
(Amendment) Act, 2023 Islands
I ASSENT
(Sgd.) John Rankin CMG,
Governor.
20th March, 2023
VIRGIN ISLANDS
No. 14 of 2023
AN ACT TO AMEND THE CRIMINAL JUSTICE (INTERNATIONAL CO-
OPERATION) ACT, REVISED EDITION 2020, AND FOR OTHER MATTERS
CONNECTED THEREWITH.
[Gazetted 21st March, 2023]
ENACTED by the Legislature of the Virgin Islands as follows:
Short title and commencement
1. (1) This Act may be cited as the Criminal Justice (International Co-
operation (Amendment) Act, 2023.
(2) This Act shall come into force on such date as the Governor may, by
Proclamation published in the Gazette, appoint.
Section 4 amended
2. Section 4 of the Criminal Justice (International Co-operation) Act, Revised
Edition 2020, as amended by the Criminal Justice (International Co-operation Act,
No. 32 of 2021, (hereinafter referred to as the “principal Act”), is amended
(a) by inserting after subsection (5), the following subsection:
“(5A) Where the Attorney General issues a letter of
request in accordance with this section, he or she and any other
person who is involved in the preparation or sending of the letter
of request shall keep the fact of the letter of request being issued or
sent, together with all related documents, confidential and secure.”;
and
(b) by inserting after subsection (7), the following subsection:
“(8) Where, pursuant to a letter of request issued in
accordance with this section, any information, document or other
material is received, the Attorney General shall, if requested in
writing by the authority that provided the information, document
or other material, provide in a timely manner feedback to that
authority on the use and usefulness of the information, document
or other material received.”.
2

Section 5 amended
3. Section 5 of the principal Act is amended by inserting after subsection (4),
the following subsections:
“(4A) Where a request for assistance from a court or tribunal or
other authority outside the Virgin Islands is received in accordance with
this section, the fact of the request being received, acted upon or
otherwise, together with all related documents, shall be kept confidential
and secure.
(4B) Where the Governor, pursuant to a request for assistance
in accordance with this section, provides any assistance to a court or
tribunal or other authority outside the Virgin Islands, he or she may in
writing and after consultation with the Attorney General, request that
court or tribunal or other authority to provide in a timely manner
feedback on the use and usefulness of the assistance provided.”.
Section 5B repealed
4. Section 5B of the principal Act is repealed.
Section 6 repealed
5. Section 6 of the principal Act is repealed.
Section 11 amended
6. Section 11 of the principal Act is amended by repealing subsection (1) and
substituting the following subsection:
“(1) It is an offence for a person to
(a) manufacture a scheduled substance;
(b) be in possession of a scheduled substance; or
(c) supply a scheduled substance to another person,
knowing or suspecting that the scheduled substance is to be used in or for
the unlawful production of a controlled drug.”.
Passed by the House of Assembly this 10th day of March, 2023.
(Sgd.) Corine N. George-Massicote,
Speaker.
(Sgd.) Phyllis Evans,
Clerk of the House of Assembly.
3

VIRGIN ISLANDS

VIRGIN ISLANDS
No. 32 of 2021
An Act to amend the Criminal Justice (International Cooperation) (Amendment) Act,
1993 (No. 8 of 1993) and to provide for other matters connected therewith.

sections

“Establishment 10A. (1) There is established a Council to
and be known as the Council of Competent Authorities
composition of
(referred to in this section and sections 10B and
Council of
10C as “the Council”).
Competent
Authorities.
(2) The Council comprises the
following membership
(a) the Managing Director of the
Financial Services
Commission, as Chairperson;
(b) the Attorney General, as
Deputy Chairperson;
(c) the Director of the Financial
Investigation Agency;
(d) the Director of the
International Tax Authority;
and
(e) a representative of the
Governor’s Office, appointed
by the Governor.
(3) The Governor may at any time
revoke the appointment of a representative
under subsection (2) (e) and replace the
representative with such other person as the
Governor may determine.
3

(4) Every member of the Council
shall endeavour to attend and participate in all
meetings of the Council, but where a member
is, for any reason, unable to attend and
participate, he or she shall designate a senior
officer (“designated person”) of his or her
office to attend and participate as his or her
representative.
(5) For the purposes of subsection
(4), a designated person shall –
(a) be the person to attend and
participate in meetings of the
Council in the absence of the
member; and
(b) have all the powers of the
member he or she is
representing as if he or she
were such member.
(6) Subsection (5) shall not be a bar
to a designated person attending a meeting of the
Council when the member is also attending and
participating in the meeting, but shall not
exercise the powers of the member.
10B. (1) The Council shall be
Functions and
powers of the responsible for –
Council.
(a) setting appropriate policies
and standards which
competent authorities may
use to implement their
respective mandates as
provided in this Act and other
enactments governing their
affairs;
(b) coordinating the activities of
the competent authorities by
holding regular meetings to
foster cooperation on law
enforcement, regulatory and
4

tax matters, including all other
related issues;
(c) exploring areas of
convergence on issues of
international cooperation as
they affect or relate to the
Virgin Islands and determine
or recommend appropriate
measures to facilitate the
Virgin Islands’ cooperation
on, and implementation of,
such issues;
(d) preparing and submitting
periodic reports to the
National Anti-money
Laundering and Terrorist
Financing Coordinating
Council established under
section 26B of the Proceeds of
Criminal Conduct Act, 1997 –
(i) on identified needs and
reforms as they pertain to
each competent authority;
and
(ii) in relation to the efficient
and effective functioning
of the Virgin Islands’
overall domestic and
international cooperation
regimes;
(e) providing technical assistance
to its members for purposes of
ensuring the efficient and
effective implementation of
their respective mandates
under this Act and other
enactments governing their
affairs;
5

(f) initiating and providing
training for staff of the
competent authorities and
other persons considered
relevant by the Council on
mutual legal assistance and
other domestic and
international cooperation
matters;
(g) reviewing, monitoring and
undertaking risk assessment
on money laundering, terrorist
financing and proliferation
financing matters as they
relate to international
cooperation; and
(h) performing such other tasks as
are consistent with the
mandates of the competent
authorities in the areas of law
enforcement, financial
regulation and supervision,
tax and domestic and
international cooperation.
(2) The Council may, for the
purposes of performing its functions under
subsection (1) –
(a) co-opt any staff of a
competent authority that the
Council considers qualified
and competent to participate
in the proceedings of the
Council to provide necessary
technical and such other
advice as the Council may
determine; and
(b) form such sub-committees as
it considers fit to perform such
specific tasks on behalf of the
6

Council as the Council may
determine in writing.
(3) Where the Council exercises any of its
powers under subsection (2), the exercise of the power
shall be on such terms and conditions as the Council may
determine.
Proceedings of 10C. (1) The quorum of the Council for the
the Council. purposes of any meeting shall be three.
(2) In the performance of its functions under
section 10B (1), the Council –
(a) shall determine its own rules of
procedure;
(b) shall determine the time and place at
which to hold its meetings, except
that the Council shall meet not less
than once in every quarter;
(c) shall determine its own agenda; and
(d) may delegate the performance of any
such functions, except that any
decision related to the outcome of
the performance of those functions
shall be taken by the Council.”.
8. Section 24 of the principal Act is amended by deleting the words Section 24
“Director of Public Prosecutions” and substituting the words “Attorney General”. amended
9. Schedule 1 of the principal Act is amended in paragraph 4 (1) by Schedule 1
deleting the words “Director of Public Prosecutions” and substituting the words amended.
“Attorney General”.
Passed by the House of Assembly this 24th day of June, 2021.
(Sgd.) Julian Willock,
Speaker.
(Sgd.) Phyllis Evans,
Clerk of the House of Assembly.
7

title: Criminal Code (Amendment) Act, 2021
text:
No. 23 of 2021
VIRGIN ISLANDS
CRIMINAL CODE (AMENDMENT) ACT, 2021
ARRANGEMENT OF SECTIONS
Section
1… Short title and commencement.
2… New section 236A inserted.
3… Schedule 1 amended.

No. 23 of 2021 Criminal Code (Amendment) Virgin
Act, 2021 Islands
I Assent
(Sgd.) John J. Rankin CMG
Governor.
14th June, 2021
VIRGIN ISLANDS
No. 23 of 2021
An Act to amend the Criminal Code Act, 1997 (No. 1 of 1997) and to provide for other
matters connected therewith.
[Gazetted 22nd June, 2021]
ENACTED by the Legislature of the Virgin Islands as follows:
Short title and 1. (1) This Act may be cited as the Criminal Code (Amendment) Act,
commencement. 2021.
(2) This Act shall come into force on such date as the Governor may, by
Proclamation published in the Gazette, appoint.
New section 2. The Criminal Conduct Code Act, 1997 (hereinafter referred to as the “principal
236A inserted. Act”) is amended by inserting immediately after section 236, the following new section –
“Intent to defraud 236A. (1) A person commits an offence if he or she,
the Government. with intent to defraud the Government, wilfully –
(a) makes, delivers or causes false or
fraudulent information to be made to a
person employed in the public service
relating to the collection of money for
the purposes of general revenue;
(b) omits information required to be
provided to a person employed in the
2

public service relating to the collection
of money for the purposes of general
revenue, where required by law; or
(c) obstructs, hinders, intimidates or resists
a person employed in the public service
in the collection of money for the
purposes of general revenue.
(2) A person who commits an offence under
subsection (1) is liable on conviction on indictment to a
term of imprisonment not exceeding 10 years or a fine not
exceeding $500,000 or both.”.
Schedule 1 3. Schedule 1 of the principal Act is amended by inserting after the row comprising
amended. section 236, the following new row –
“236A Intent to defraud 10 years A ”.
Government $500,000
Passed by the House of Assembly this 11th day of May, 2021.
(Sgd.) Julian Willock,
Speaker.
(Sgd.) Phyllis Evans,
Clerk of the House of Assembly.
3
